design_philosophy.core_loop: One-on-one fighting matches where players deplete opponent health bars through special moves and basic attacks; customizable six-button control scheme enables diverse combat execution
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Direct player-vs-player mechanical skill: landing special move combos, managing positioning, and adapting to opponent patterns in real-time combat
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Control iconic Dragon Ball Z characters in authentic one-on-one duels, mirroring the series' signature martial arts combat philosophy
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately modeled after Street Fighter II rather than creating a unique fighting game language; producer Toshihiro Suzuki chose fighting game genre specifically due to personal preference and genre popularity, establishing a clear design intent to capitalize on the fighting game boom while leveraging Dragon Ball IP

difficulty.continues_system: Continue system present (evidenced by soundtrack track 'Theme of Continue'), suggesting arcade-style progression with limited continues
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Implicit difficulty scaling through character roster selection and opponent AI progression across arcade-style tournament bracket structure

progression.unlock_mechanism: One additional character transformation unlockable via cheat code; base roster of 10 playable characters (Goku, Piccolo, Vegeta, Frieza, Android 20, Android 18, Android 16, Cell, Trunks, Gohan) with individual character transformations
replayability_hooks: Multiple playable characters with distinct move sets and transformations
Three playable modes supporting diverse play patterns
Multiplayer competitive matches enabling head-to-head play
Hidden unlockable transformation via cheat code providing discovery incentive

world_building_techniques: Character selection roster drawn from Dragon Ball Z anime canon, allowing player familiarity to establish narrative context
Stage themes named after characters (Piccolo, Vegeta, Frieza, Android 20, Android 18, Cell, Android 16), embedding narrative through environmental audio design
Arcade tournament bracket structure implicitly mirrors Dragon Ball Z tournament narrative arc without explicit storytelling

faction_indicators: Character alignment inherent to Dragon Ball Z source material: heroes (Goku, Piccolo, Gohan, Trunks) versus villains (Vegeta, Frieza, Androids, Cell)
Transformations signify power escalation, mirroring series narrative hierarchy
danger_signals: Android characters (20, 18, 16) represent existential threats in source material
Cell as final boss character embodies narrative culmination threat
Frieza as inter-dimensional conqueror establishes external danger archetype
environmental_storytelling: Battle stage themes attributed to specific character origins, creating implicit location associations
Arcade tournament structure echoes Dragon Ball Z martial arts tournament narrative tradition
Character roster sequencing (Piccolo → Vegeta → Frieza → Androids → Cell) mirrors approximate narrative progression of source anime

sources_used: wikipedia: Dragon Ball Z: Super Butōden gameplay section, development section, and character roster documentation
